coredata.dc:title: Boosted photocatalytic activity by depositing carbon quantum dots as electron mediator and photothermal-assisted site in dual heat source-based sponge

coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQD) and CoO nanoparticles were loaded as double heat sources in the hollow porous sponge g-C3N4 with photothermal effect, and the CQDs/CoO/PMSCN Z-scheme heterojunction photothermal assisted photocatalytic system was constructed. Meanwhile, a series of characterization and DFT calculations were used to reveal the electron transfer mode inside the heterojunction. The photocatalytic degradation activity was enhanced by coupling the photothermal effect of multiple heat sources with the electron transfer in the heterojunction, and CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN exhibited a TC degradation rate of 94.7 % after 90 min of light reaction. Notably, after five cycles of experimentation, the degradation rate decreased by only 4 %, demonstrating the material’s good stability and reusability. Additionally, the unique three-dimensional hollow porous structure of PMSCN allows incident light to penetrate and undergoes multiple reflections/refractions. This not only improves the light response range, but also provides certain thermal insulation, reducing heat loss. Finally, the ECOSAR assessment and bean sprout experiments indicated that TC degradation proceeded towards lower toxicity and non-toxicity. These findings highlight the effectiveness of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN for the treatment of antibiotic contamination, maximize energy utilization, and offer new insights for the practical application of CQDs.

head.ce:title: Boosted photocatalytic activity by depositing carbon quantum dots as electron mediator and photothermal-assisted site in dual heat source-based sponge

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Water Resource and Environment, Hebei Province Key Laboratory of Sustained Utilization & Development of Water Recourse, Hebei Center for Ecological and Environmental Geology Research, Hebei Geo University, Shijiazhuang 050031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQD) and CoO nanoparticles were loaded as double heat sources in the hollow porous sponge g-C3N4 with photothermal effect, and the CQDs/CoO/PMSCN Z-scheme heterojunction photothermal assisted photocatalytic system was constructed. Meanwhile, a series of characterization and DFT calculations were used to reveal the electron transfer mode inside the heterojunction. The photocatalytic degradation activity was enhanced by coupling the photothermal effect of multiple heat sources with the electron transfer in the heterojunction, and CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN exhibited a TC degradation rate of 94.7 % after 90 min of light reaction. Notably, after five cycles of experimentation, the degradation rate decreased by only 4 %, demonstrating the material’s good stability and reusability. Additionally, the unique three-dimensional hollow porous structure of PMSCN allows incident light to penetrate and undergoes multiple reflections/refractions. This not only improves the light response range, but also provides certain thermal insulation, reducing heat loss. Finally, the ECOSAR assessment and bean sprout experiments indicated that TC degradation proceeded towards lower toxicity and non-toxicity. These findings highlight the effectiveness of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN for the treatment of antibiotic contamination, maximize energy utilization, and offer new insights for the practical application of CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid economic development, the misuse of antibiotics has become increasingly severe, particularly concerning water pollution, which is now a global environmental [1]. Tetracycline (TC), the most widely used antibiotic, is known for its high biotoxicity, challenging biodegradation, and potential to induce drug resistance. Its prolonged accumulation in surface and groundwater can lead to the growth of bacteria with drug-resistant genes. This not only threatens the stability of ecosystems, but also poses a significant risk to human health [2,3]. photocatalytic technology has emerged as a promising “green” method for addressing TC pollution in water environments. It offers mild reaction conditions, minimal secondary pollution, high efficiency, and the direct use of solar energy to degrade organic pollutants [4]. However, the effectiveness of photocatalysis is hindered by low catalyst energy conversion efficiency and carrier separation rates [5], overlooking the significant potential of near-infrared light(NIR). In recent years, researchers have proposed photothermal assisted photocatalysis strategies, which can multiple-fields coupling to increase the temperature of the system and accelerate the chemical reaction kinetics on the catalyst surface without consuming external energy. Wang et al.[6] showed that Ag/α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 could convert part of the absorbed photon energy into thermal energy through the photothermal effect during the photocatalysis process. The composite exhibited excellent performance in photocatalytic hydrogen production (3125.62 μmol⋅g−1⋅h−1) and tetracycline degradation (93.6 %). Shi et al.[7] constructed a black g-C3N4/ chitosan (CN-B/CS) composite hydrogel system with the synergistic effect of photothermal assisted photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics and solar interfacial evaporation. The catalyst was able to degrade 82.5 % of tetracycline (TC) solution (100 mg/L) degradation within 4 h of solar irradiation. Therefore, Finding photocatalytic materials with good light-absorbing and photothermal conversion capabilities is essential for achieving full-spectrum solar energy utilization [8–10].

Currently, most photothermal materials and photocatalysts can only increase the temperature of the composite system by realizing their own effect or introducing a single heat source, and all the monomers in these photothermal assisted photocatalytic systems cannot ensure that they can effectively and fully utilize sunlight to accelerate the photocatalytic degradation rate[11,12]. In this paper, a hollow porous network structure floating sponge g-C3N4 (PMSCN) with photothermal effect is synthesized by combining low-cost melamine sponge with urea by one-step heat shrinkage method, and dual-heat-source materials: CoO nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), were loaded onto PMSCN to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction photothermal-assisted photocatalytic system based on multiple heat sources. Compared with other studies (Table S1), this system achieved ‘1 + 1 + 1 > 3′, and maximized the antibiotic degradation efficiency and energy utilisation. Firstly, this floating structure provides more catalytic active sites and effectively solves the problem of irreversible accumulation of ordinary powder g-C3N4 in water [13,14]. When the incident light enters into the hollow PMSCN, it undergoes multiple reflection and scattering, and the temperature gradually increases. Meanwhile, the structure has a certain thermal insulation effect to reduce the heat loss [15,16]. Secondly, the Z-scheme heterojunction constructed by interfacial regulation not only fully address the issue of high recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, which is caused by the Coulomb force between electrons and holes in a single semiconductor material like PMSCN, but also retains the strong redox potential of CoO and PMSCN, and accelerates the photothermal assisted photocatalytic reaction kinetics. Thirdly, the CQDs have unique Π-Π conjugate structures and rich functional groups, which can be used as electronic media and photothermal auxiliary sites to further enhance the photothermal effect of composite materials [17–19]. Research shows that the ultra-thin g-C3N4 modified with carbon quantum dots removed 100 % diclofenac within 6 min under broad-spectrum light irradiation [20]. However, relatively few studies have explored the photothermal effects of CQDs as a “heat source” in photocatalytic reactions, which may provide deeper insights into the charge transfer mechanism at the semiconductor interface in the presence of CQDs. Finally, the strong absorption of light by PMSCN, CoO and CQDs was used to enrich the active site of photothermal materials[21,22]. With the enhancement of the photothermal effect of multiple heat sources, this unique photothermal assisted photocatalytic system exhibits superior photothermal conversion efficiency than a single photothermal material [23].

Herein, the floating CQDs/CoO/PMSCN Z-scheme heterojunction with hollow porous structure were designed and synthesized through a simple heat shrinkage and solvothermal process. The strong absorption of light by CoO, CQDs and PMSCN three heat sources was used to improve the energy conversion efficiency of photogenerated electrons. This hollow sponge structure not only exhibits enhanced specific surface area and adsorption active site, but also has a certain thermal insulation effect. Characterization and DFT calculations revealed the electron transfer mode of the Z--scheme heterojunction, and the photocatalytic activity and photothermal effects of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN were evaluated through TC degradation under visible light. Meanwhile, the possible reaction pathway and photocatalytic mechanism for TC degradation by CQDs/CoO/PMSCN were proposed based on trapping experiments and the identification of intermediates. Finally, the experimental results were analyzed to reveal the mechanism of boosting the catalytic degradation activity by coupling the multi-heat sources photothermal effect and Z-scheme heterojunctions to build photothermal-assisted photocatalytic system.

Urea(CH4N2O) and melamine were purchased from Chengdu Akeda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Vitamin C (VC), thiobarbituric acid (TBA), and triethanolamine(TEOA) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrrolineN-oxide (DMPO) was purchased from DOJINDO Laboratories. All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The CQDs were synthesized using an electrochemical method to etch graphite rods [24]. Initially, two graphite rods were submerged in ultra-pure water, serving as the anode and cathode respectively. A bias voltage of 30 V was applied between the electrodes. After 10 days of continuous stirring, the solution gradually turned black The resulting solution was then filtered twice and freeze-dried into a powder for further use.

Firstly, 16 g of urea and 8 g of melamine were solubilized in 40 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 3 h. Subsequently, melamine sponges were cut into 4 × 2 × 2 cm3 pieces and soaked in the saturated urea solution until the weight of urea absorbed by the sponge was 70 times that of the original melamine sponge. After freeze-drying for 24 h, the sponge was transferred to a semi-open ceramic crucible and heated to 550 ℃ at a rate of 5°C/min in a N2 atmosphere for 4 h. The resulting g-C3N4 with a hollow porous mesh structure was designated as PMSCN. A separate sponge calcinated without further treatment and was labeled as C-MS.

The preparation procedure of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN was illustrated in Fig. 1 a First, 0.92 g of cobalt acetate was dissolved in a mixture of 32 mL of n-octanol and 8 mL of ethanol, and the solution was stirred on a constant-temperature magnetic stirrer for two hours. Then, 0.117 g of PMSCN and a specified amount of CQDs (1, 2, 3 mg) were added, followed by ultrasonication for 40 min.The resulting mixturewas then poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220 ℃ for 4 h. The prepared catalysts were washed three times with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the CQDs/CoO/PMSCN with a porous network structure was collected. Based on the amount of CQDs used, the samples were named as CQDs-1/CoO/PMSCN, CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs-3/CoO/PMSCN, respectively. CoO/PMSCN was prepared using the same method but without adding CQDs.

For comparison, a fixed mass of urea and melamine was calcined in N2 at 550 °C for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 °C /min to obtain powdered g-C3N4. Subsequently, g-C3N4 was used in place of PMSCN and CQDs-2/CoO/CN was prepared using the same method.

The crystal structures of CoO, PMSCN, CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs/CoO/PMSCN powders were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a SmartLab 9 kW system, over a 2θ range of 20° to 85°. The diffraction parameters were as follows: X-ray source CuKα (λ = 0.15406 nm), voltage 40 kV, current 40 mA, step size 0.02°, scan rate 20°/min. Fourier-transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Thermo Fisher Scientific iS20 spectrometer, the wavelength range was 400 to 4000 cm−1. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI SU5000) was used to observe the morphological characteristics of the catalyst. The vacuum was maintained at 10-4 to 10-5 Torr in the sample chamber, and a voltage of 10 ∼ 20 kV was applied to improve the definition of the field of view. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Japan-JEOL-JEM 2100F) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) were used to characterize the microstructure of the materials. The measurement methods: bright field; Acceleration voltage: 200 kV. The chemical composition and valence states of the prepared photocatalysts were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi), the X-ray source was Al Kα (hv = 1486.8 Ev), the vacuum was 8 × l0-10 Pa, the operating voltage was 12.5 kV, the filament current was 16 mA, the passband energy was 40 eV, and step size was 0.1 eV. The spectra were corrected with standard C1s = 284.6 eV, and the Avantage software was used for peak-splitting fitting. The Multi-Channel Photochemical Reaction System PCX-50C from PerfectLight was employed for conducting the photothermal-photocatalytic reactions. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra of the materials were measured using a UV-2550 spectrometer, the test wavelength ranges from 200 nm to 850 nm. Steady-state tests were conducted using the luminescence spectrometer (FLS980), while photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with the fluorescence spectrometer (HITACHI F4600). BET surface area and pore size analyses were performed using the ASAP2460 multi-station fully automatic analyzer from Micromeritics (United States), the degassing temperature was 150 °C and the degassing time was 8 h, N2 was filled and the test temperature was 77 K. Electron spin resonance (ESR) for ·O2 – and ·OH was carried out with a Bruker A300. The transient photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (MS) analyses were performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E), and a PLS-FX300HU xenon lamp was used for the experiments. Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS) spectra were collected on a Thermo ESCALAB XI + with He I (21.22 eV) excitation line, and the total acquisition time was 1 min 30.1 s. Scan count: 3; Analyzer mode: CA: Pass Energy 1.0 eV; Energy step size: 0.05 eV; Number of energy steps: 601. Photocatalytic reaction intermediates and degradation pathways were analyzed using ICP-MS (Agilent 7700, US). Additionally, the relevant parameters for density functional theory calculations are presented in the Supporting Information.

To assess the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN for tetracycline degradation, 20 mg each of CoO, PMSCN, CoO/PMSCN, CoO/PMSCN, CQDs-1/CoO/PMSCN, CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs-3/CoO/PMSCN was added to 50 mL of TC solution with a concentration of 40 mg/L. The reaction was allowed to proceed in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. A 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the light source, and the sample was irradiated for 90 min. Every 15 min, 3 mL of solution was collected and separated by high-speed centrifugation at 7000 r/min. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 357 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The TC degradation rate in the experiments was determined using the following Eq.(1):
(1)
η =
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100 %
where C0 and Ct represent the concentrations of TC at the initial reaction times and at time t, respectively. Na2CO3 and NaHCO3 of different concentrations were added to 40 mg/L TC to investigate the inorganic ions on TC degradation.

To evaluate the stability and repeatability of the photocatalyst, five consecutive cycle experiments were conducted. After each reaction, the photocatalysts were collected, washed with ultrapure water and ethanol, dried, and then analyzed using XRD. Finally, the TC degradation experiments were performed under outdoor sunlight to assess the practical application of the photocatalysts in a controlled settings.

5 mg of PMSCN was weighed and added to 1 mL of C2H5OH, then sonicated to create a uniform solution. This mixture was evenly coated onto clean, dry ITO conductive glass, dried, and the coating process was repeated 3 times to prepare the working electrode. Electrochemical tests were conducted using a CHI 710B workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.). The electrolyte used was a 0.5 M sodium sulfate solution, with Ag/AgCl and platinum sheet electrodes serving as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively.

The intermediates produced during TC photodegradation were analyzed using the Ultimate 300UHPLC-Q Exactive from Thermo Scientific, USA. The mobile phase consisted of a mixture of acetonitrile and 0.1 % formic acid solution, with a flow rate of 0.25 mL/min. The capillary temperature was set to 300℃, the probe heating temperature to 350 ℃, and the spraying voltage to 3200 V. The detector wavelength range was 190 to 400 nm.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were measured using a Bruker ER200-SRC spectrometer with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrole N-oxide (DMPO) as a sacrificial agent to identify active species. To investigate the effects of ·O2 –, ·OH, and h+ on TC degradation, free radical scavenging experiments were conducted by adding 1 mmol each of vitamin C (VC), thiobutyric acid (TBA), and triethanolamine (TEOA) to 50 mL of 40 mg/L TC solution.

The morphology, microstructure, and elemental composition of CoO/PMSCN and CQDs/CoO/PMSCN were characterized by SEM and EDS. As shown in Fig. 1b-d, PMSCN exhibited a three-dimensional porous mesh structure with a hollow interior. CoO nanoparticles and g-C3N4 sheets were evenly distributed on the surface of PMSCN, enhancing the photocatalyst’s specific surface area and light absorbance. This structure also provided thermal insulation, effectively improving the photothermal co-catalytic performance [25]. To confirm the loading of CQDs on PMSCN, the surface material composition of 2-CQDs/CoO/PMSCN was further analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1e, the CQDs were uniformly dispersed across the surface of the material, with most having diameters below 500 nm. Moreover, CoO nanoparticles were immobilized on the surface of the porous PMSCN structure, enhancing the number of photocatalytic active sites. In the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 1f, the 0.25 nm lattice spacing corresponds to the (111) crystallographic plane of CoO [26,27], while the 0.32 nm lattice spacing aligns with the (002) crystallographic plane of CQDs [28]. This confirms the successful incorporation of CQDs into the CQDs/CoO/PMSCN composite. More importantly, the highly crystalline and small-sized CQDs serve as active sites for photo-thermal catalytic degradation of pollutants [29]. Additionally, the elemental distribution plots (Fig. 1g-h) and EDS (Fig. S1) indicated the presence of four elements (C, N, O, and Co) in the composites. These findings further confirm the successful construction of the CQDs/CoO/PMSCN heterojunction.

The crystal structures of CoO, PMSCN, CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs-CoO/PMSCN were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1i, the characteristic diffraction peaks of pure CoO at 2θ values of 36.5°, 42.4°, 61.5°, 73.6°, and 77.5° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (322) lattice planes of the cubic phase CoO (JCPDS 71–1178) [30], respectively. The characteristic diffraction peak of PMSCN at 27.5° corresponded to g-C3N4, while peaks at 37.6°, 43.9°, 64.3°, and 77.5° were attributed to C-MS. With the introduction of CQDs, the diffraction peaks of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN were nearly identical to those of CoO/PMSCN, likely due to the low content and diffraction intensity of CQDs [31]. Additionally, the diffraction peak of C-MS was not observed in any of the XRD patterns of the composites, which can be attributed to the CQDs and CoO covering the surface of PM SCN, thus obscuring the C-MS [32,33].

The chemical compositions and molecular structures of CoO, PMSCN, CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs/CoO/PMSCN were analyzed using FTIR, as shown in Fig. 1j. The distinctive peak in the range of 2270–2400 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching of asymmetric C = O bonds, attributed to various suspension bonds formed on the surface of the nanoparticles [34,35]. The main characteristic peaks of PMSCN were similar to those of CoO/PMSCN, but their intensities showed some deviations due to the presence of CoO. Additionally, the FTIR spectra of CoO/PMSCN and CQDs/CoO/PSMCN were similar. The peaks in the ranges of 1200–1800 cm−1 and 600–1000 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C = N bonds and the vibration mode of the triazine unit, respectively [36–38]. This indicates that the original structure of CoO/PSMCN remained largely unchanged after the addition of a small amount of CQDs, consistent with the XRD results. In summary, CQDs/CoO/PMSCN exhibits all characteristic peaks of CoO and PMSCN, confirming the successful synthesis of the composite.

The CQDs/CoO/PMSCN composite featured a unique three-dimensional hollow network structure with abundant porous properties. To analyze these properties, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (under 77.381 K liquid nitrogen conditions) were used to examine different ratios of PMSCN, CoO, and CQDs/CoO/PMSCN. As shown in Fig. 1k, CoO exhibited a type II isotherm, while the other materials showed type IV isotherms, consistent with the characteristics of porous materials [39,40]. The specific surface areas of PMSCN, CoO, CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN were 93.7934 m2·g−1, 3.8572 m2·g−1, 434.3935 m2·g−1, and 33.9210 m2·g−1, respectively. The specific surface area of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN increases with the addition of more carbon dots. This is due to the porous nature of CQDs, which have a large specific surface area and high adsorption capacity. A higher BET surface area provides more active sites and greater adsorption of active substances, enhancing photocatalytic activity [31]. However, the specific surface area of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN was smaller than that of PMSCN alone. This decrease was likely because the incorporation of CoO nanoparticles and CQDs led to pore blockage and agglomeration, which affected the morphological features of PMSCN to some extent [41].

The elemental composition, chemical states, and interfacial interactions of the catalysts before and after the addition of CQDs were characterized using XPS. The full XPS spectra (Fig. S2) exhibited that the main elements in the materials were C, N, O, and Co. The increased intensity of the C peaks in CQDs/CoO/PMSCN compared to CoO/PMSCN indicates successful loading of CQDs onto PMSCN, which is consistent with the TEM and HRTEM results. High-resolution XPS was further employed to analyze the chemical composition of the catalysts. Fig. 4a shows the Co 2p spectrum, where the Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 spin–orbit peaks of CoO are located at 796.6 eV and 780.8 eV, respectively. Two distinct satellite peaks at 786.0 eV and 802.9 eV suggest that Co is present in the form of Co2+ in cobalt oxides [42]. In Fig. 2 b, the O 1 s spectrum displays three peaks, indicating the presence of oxygen in three different chemical states. The peak at 529.6 eV in the O 1 s spectrum confirms the presence of lattice oxygen in the Co-O bond, while the peak at 531.6 eV corresponds to the Co-O-C bond. The peak at 532.4 eV was attributed to C-O oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the composite [43,44]. Additionally, the C 1 s spectrum of the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN complex (Fig. 2c) exhibits three major peaks at 288.5 eV, 286.5 eV, and 284.7 eV, representing N-C = N, C-N, and C = C bonds, respectively [45]. In Fig. 2d, the N 1 s peak at 388.5 eV is attributed to the sp2 hybridized aromatic N bonded to a C atom (C = N-C). The binding energy positions at 400.8 eV and 399.5 eV correspond to the amino group and tertiary nitrogen N-(C)3 group, respectively [46]. The observed shift of binding energies and peak intensities to higher values in CQDs/CoO/PMSCN compared to CoO/PMSCN indicates enhanced interfacial interactions and charge transfer between CQDs, CoO, and PMSCN.

Fig. 2e and Fig. S3a showed the structure model of PMSCN and CoO. Furthermore, the energy band structures of PMSCN and CoO were calculated by density functional theory (DFT)(Fig. 2f and Fig. S3b), the energy band gap of PMSCN was 1.432 eV. The Fermi level of CoO spans the gap band with a gap width of 0, indicating that CoO had metal abundance and strong conductivity, which makes it ideal for constructing heterostructures with PMSCN. Fig. 2g and Fig. S3c showed the density of states (DOS) plots of the two monomers, indicating the degree of material contribution to the orbitals. The Fermi levels of all samples fall within the DOS zero region, confirming their semiconducting properties. The analysis showed that the CBM and VBM of PMSCN were mainly controlled by the p track. The CBM of CoO was mainly from the p track, the VBM was dominated by the d track.

The light absorption properties of the catalysts were examined using UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3
a, CoO not only absorbed UV light but also exhibited an absorption peak in the visible range. The strong light absorption ability of PMSCN was attributed to its dark black color. After combining CoO with PMSCN, the absorption spectrum of the material showed a noticeable red-shift. This red shift is primarily due to the combination of the two materials, which facilitates the transfer of photogenerated carriers, thereby significantly enhancing the light utilization efficiency [14]. It was noteworthy that after the introduction of CQDs, the composite exhibited strong absorption in both the UV and visible regions, indicating that CQDs have an efficient light-harvesting ability and can broaden the spectral response range [47,48]. Additionally, as shown in the Tauc plot derived from the Kubelka-Munk function (Fig. S4), the band gaps of CoO and PMSCN were 2.35 eV and 1.60 eV, respectively. The Mott-Schottky (MS) test was further applied to determine the band structure of the material. As shown in Fig. 3b, the flat-band potentials (EFB) of CoO and PMSCN were 0.73 V and −0.55 V (vs. NHE), respectively. The negative slope of the CoO linear curve and the positive slope of the PMSCN curve indicate that CoO is a p-type semiconductor, while PMSCN is an n-type semiconductor. It is important to note that the FB is typically 0.1 V higher than the conduction band (CB) in n-type semiconductors and 0.1 V lower than the valence band in p-type semiconductors [41]. Therefore, the valence band of CoO and the conduction band of PMSCN were determined to be 0.83 V and −0.65 V, respectively (vs. NHE). Finally, based on the Eg measurement results and using the equation EVB = Eg + ECB [49], the conduction band of CoO was calculated to be −1.52 V, and the valence band of PMSCN was calculated to be 0.95 V. To further investigate the interfacial charge transfer mechanism, the work function of the monomer samples was measured using UPS. As shown in Fig. 3c, the cut-off energies for CoO and PMSCN were 16.95 eV and 18.11 eV, respectively. By subtracting these cut-off energies from the He I excitation energy (21.22 eV), the work functions were determined to be 4.27 eV and 3.11 eV for CoO and PMSCN, respectively [50,51].

Electrochemical testing can evaluate the separation performance of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 3d showed the photocurrent responses of CoO, PMSCN, CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN under intermittent visible light irradiation for four switching cycles. The photocurrent response intensity of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN was notably the highest, indicating that the addition of CQDs effectively enhances the separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [52]. Additionally, the Nyquist plot (Fig. 3e) shows that the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN composite has the smallest curvature radius, indicating the fastest interfacial charge transfer rate [53]. To further analyze the recombination of photogenerated carriers in the catalyst, PL spectra of the samples were tested, as shown in Fig. 3f CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN exhibited the lowest fluorescence intensity compared to CoO, PMSCN, and CoO/PMSCN, indicating that CQDs help inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers [54,55]. This PL spectrum analysis was consistent with the results from electrochemical measurements, impedance studies, and photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, the CQDs/CoO/PMSCN composite exhibits a fast electron transfer rate and efficient carrier separation, enhancing its participation in the photocatalytic reaction process.

Fig. 4a illustrated the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of TC (40 mg/L) using PMSCN, CoO, PMSCN/CoO, and CQDs/CoO/PMSCN under visible light irradiation. Initially, the pollutants and photocatalysts were mixed and stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Following this, photocatalytic experiments were conducted under visible light for 90 min. The results showed that the degradation rate of TC was negligible in the absence of a photocatalyst. The degradation rates of TC by PMSCN, CoO, and CoO/PMSCN were 71.2 %, 53.9 %, and 87 %, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation activity of the composites was further enhanced by introducing CQDs to form CQDs/CoO/PMSCN heterojunctions. The degradation rates of CQDs-1/CoO/PMSCN, CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs-3/CoO/PMSCN were 90.2 %, 94.7 %, and 92.4 %, respectively, with CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN demonstrating the highest degradation efficiency. Additionally, the degradation curve of TC is well described by the quasi-first-order model, with the reaction rate constant (k) expressed as ln(C0/C) = kt (Fig. 4b-c) [56]. The CQD-2/CoO/PMSCN heterojunction exhibited the highest rate constant (0.0288 min−1), which was 2.33, 3.65, and 1.48 times greater than those of PMSCN (0.01237 min−1), CoO (0.0788 min−1), and CoO/PMSCN (0.0144 min−1), respectively. The reaction rate constant of CQDs-3/CoO/PMSCN (0.02564 min−1) was lower than that of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN. This reduction is attributed to the excessive accumulation of CQDs on the surface of the CoO/PMSCN binary composite, which hinders charge transfer and reduces the availability of active sites, ultimately decreasing photocatalytic efficiency [57]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the photocatalytic degradation activity of the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN heterojunction remained relatively stable after five cycles, with only a 4.7 % decrease in degradation rate, demonstrating good stability and recyclability. The XRD spectra (Fig. 4e) of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN after five cycles remained nearly unchanged compared to those before cycling. Additionally, we researched the effect of anions in natural water on photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. In Fig.S5, the photocatalytic degradation of TC was slightly inhibited by the anion (CO3 2– and HCO3 –). This phenomenon may occur due to the reaction of CO3 2– and HCO3 – with reactive free radicals (OH), resulting in the decrease in photocatalytic activity, as illustrated in the following equation[58,59]:

To further explore the organic degradation and the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of catalysts, Fig. S6 showed the degradation of COD by CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN. After 90 min of light irradiation, the COD decreased from 76.2 mg/L to 28.7 mg/L, and the COD removal rate was calculated to be 62.3 %. Obviously, the COD removal efficiency was lower than the degradation efficiency, which may be due to the fact that some intermediates are still present in the solution. Meanwhile, the AQE of CQD-2/CoO/PMSCN Z-scheme heterojunction was 0.31 % calculated by Eq.(4) [60].

Where CODI and CODf represented for the initial and final COD concentrations, respectively. I is the incident energy, h is the Planck constant 6.63 × 10−34 J⋅s, c is the speed of light 3 × 108 m/s, and N is Avogadro's constant.

Outdoor experiments were conducted to simulate the actual application environment of photocatalytic materials. Fig. 4f showed the locations of the experiments and the trend of the outdoor temperature during the reaction (date: July 6, 2024; time: 11:00–15:00; location: 38°02′N, 114°55′; temperature: 33–37 ℃). Fig. 4g showed the photocatalytic performance of the catalysts after four hours of sunlight exposure. The degradation rate for the blank control group was negligible, while CQDs/CoO/PMSCN achieved a degradation rate of 88.47 %, significantly higher than that of CoO, PMSCN, and CoO/PMSCN. Additionally, all reaction procedures adhered to quasi-primary kinetics (Fig. 4h-i), with CQDs /CoO/PMSCN exhibiting a K value as high as 0.00712 min−1.

To investigate the photothermal effect of the catalysts, IR images of the photocatalysts in TC and DIW solutions were collected during the photocatalytic experiments. Fig. 5 a clearly demonstrates that the heating rate of the catalytic system in the presence of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN was faster than that in the TC and DIW solutions. Within 10 min, the temperature of the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN + H2O solution reached 40.3 ℃, and the temperature of the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN + TC solution rose even higher to 43.6 ℃. This increase was attributed to electronic transfer within the Z-scheme heterojunction and the photothermal effect of the catalytic materials in the solution [51]. To further distinguish the contributions of these factors to catalytic activity, the photocatalytic efficiencies of CoO, PMSCN, CoO/PMSCN, and CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN for TC were investigated under two temperature conditions: controlled temperature (10 ℃) and room temperature (RT) (Fig. 5c). The degradation rates of TC using CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN at 10 ℃ and room temperature were 76 % and 94.59 %, respectively, demonstrating that photothermal effects can enhance the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. The efficiencies of CoO, PMSCN, and CoO/PMSCN at 10 ℃ were 37 %, 52.87 %, and 62.72 %, respectively, indicating that the Z-scheme heterojunction enhances catalytic activity. Fig. S7 presented the catalytic degradation curves fitted with quasi-first-order kinetic equations. Fig. 5d showed that CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN exhibited the higest catalytic degradation, with a K value of 0.0207 min−1.

These experiments showed that the photothermal effect significantly influences photocatalytic degradation activity, with temperature playing a key role in the catalytic system. Therefore, an infrared imager was used to capture temperature changes images in the samples under irradiation from a 300 W xenon lamp (Fig. 5b). The temperature of the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN powder increased from 33.9 ℃ to 104 ℃ within 180 s. In comparison, the temperatures of CoO, PMSCN, and CoO/PMSCN at 180 s were 71.7 ℃, 84.2 ℃, and 93.6 ℃, respectively. Clearly, CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN exhibited a greater temperature increase than the pristine materials and the 2D composite material, indicating that the introduction of CQDs enhances the temperature of the catalyst surface and further demonstrating the effective photothermal effect of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN.

Fig. 6 a examined the temperature changes of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN and CQDs-2/CoO/CN under a 300 W xenon lamp for 10 min on and off, respectively, comparing the heat loss of the two materials. After cooling with the lamp switched off, the temperature of CQDs-2/CoO/CN was 28.9 ℃, only 4.6 ℃ above room temperature, with an average cooling rate of approximately 1.29 ℃/min (Fig. 6b). Notably, CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN exhibited a higher average cooling rate of approximately 1.07 ℃/min. When illuminated for 10 min, the temperature of the material reached 44.2 ℃. After the light was turned off and the material was allowed to cool for 10 min, the temperature decreased to 33.6 ℃, which was 9 ℃ above room temperature. This enhanced thermal retention is attributed to the hollow structure of PMSCN, which reduces heat loss [25]. Additionally, light entering the structure is reflected/refracted, which improves the utilization of CQDs photons and contributes to the excellent thermal insulation performance of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN.

To study the degradation mechanism of the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN photocatalytic reaction system, capture experiments were conducted to detect the main active species. Fig. 7 a showed that VC, TEOA, and BQ were used as scavengers for h+, ·OH, and ·O2 –, respectively. In the presence of VC and TEOA, the decomposition of TC was significantly inhibited, with degradation efficiencies of 34.8 % and 16.67 %, respectively. TBA also strong inhibited TC degradation. The results indicated that h+, ·O2 –, and ·OH are the main active species, with h+ and ·O2 – contributing the most, followed by ·OH. Additionally, the ESR technique using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrole N-oxide (DMPO) was employed to further identify the primary free radical species in the photocatalytic process. Fig. 7b-c show no signals for ·OH and ·O2 – under dark conditions. However, after 10 min of irradiation with a xenon lamp, strong signals for ·OH and ·O2 – were detected in water and methanol solutions, respectively. These results indicate that ·O2 –, ·OH, and h+ are involved in the degradation of TC by CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN heterojunctions.

Based on the above experimental results analysis, the indirect Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/CoO/PMSCN was proposed, as displayed in Fig. 7d. The CB, VB and Ef positions of CoO were higher than those of PMSCN, and the work function of CoO was also higher than that of PMSCN, indicating that the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism was not suitable for this system[61]. Upon visible light irradiation, CoO and PMSCN are excited, generating electrons in the CB and holes in the VB, respectively. As an electronic mediator, CQDs exhibit excellent electron transfer and storage capabilities between the two semiconductors. The e- from the CB of PMSCN migrates to the VB of CoO, which prevents the recombination of the electron-hole pairs in PMSCN and CoO [62,63]. Since the CB potential of CoO (−1.52 V) is more negative than the O2/·O2 – redox potential (−0.33 V) [64], O2 reacts with the electrons generated by photoexcitation to produce ·O2 –. The resulting ·O2 – and OH radicals convert TC to CO2, H2O and NH4 +. Thus, CQDs-doped CoO/PMSCN Z-scheme heterojunctions were constructed, enhancing the catalyst’s redox ability and improving the efficiency of catalytic pollutant degradation.

LC-MS was employed to investigate the intermediates and reaction pathways involved in the degradation of TC. Fig. S6 showed the mass spectra of CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN after 90 min of photocatalytic degradation of TC. The TC solution initially displayed a peak at m/z = 445, corresponding to the protonated form of tetracycline [65]. During the photocatalytic process, approximately 12 intermediates were identified with m/z values of 415, 300, 220, 164, 79, 461, 328, 305, 150, 283, 129, and 57, respectively. Fig. 8 illustrates the specific structures of the intermediates and proposes two possible pathways for TC degradation based on previous studies. In pathway one, the main reactions involve hydroxylation and amination. Initially, the TC molecule is converted to P1 (m/z = 415) due to the disappearance of hydroxyl and amine moieties [66]. Subsequently, P1 is attacked by free radicals, leading to bond breaking and demethylation to form P2 (m/z = 300), which then undergoes additional demethylation, ring opening, and decarbonylation to produce P3 (m/z = 220). Finally, P3 undergoes further hydroxylation and ring opening, resulting in the formation of P4 (m/z = 164) and P5 (m/z = 79). Path II involves an ammonia reaction to produce P6 (m/z = 461) [66]. P6 then undergoes methylation, dehydroxylation, and decarbonylation to form P7 (m/z = 328). On one hand, P7 undergoes ring opening and double-bond cleavage to form P8 (m/z = 305), which then undergoes dehydroxylation and additional ring opening to produce P9 (m/z = 150). On the other hand, P7 loses its keto group, breaks bonds, and opens the ring to form P10 (m/z = 283). P10 then undergoes sequential reactions including demethylation, dehydroxylation, loss of carbonyl, ring opening, double-bond cleavage, and detachment of the branched chain, resulting in P11 (m/z = 129) and P12 (m/z = 57) [51,67]. Finally, during the degradation process, P5, P9, P11, and P12 are mineralized into CO2, H2O, NH4 +, and other end products.

Additionally, the ECOSAR software was used to analyze the quantitative structure-effect relationship (QSAR) of the intermediates and assess their toxicity to fish, daphnia, and algae (Fig. 9 a-b and Table S2). This approach allows for the prediction of both acute and chronic toxicity of chemical substances based on existing data. The Globally Harmonized System of Chemical Classification and Labelling (GHS) indicated that fish, Daphnia, and algae can serve as substitutes for all aquatic organisms, and that data obtained from testing these species are comparable. Compounds P1, P2, P3, P7, P10, and P11 exhibited higher acute toxicity to green algae, likely due to the presence of toxic C = C chemical bonds [41]. Generally, the toxicity levels initially increased and then decreased during the degradation process. The intermediates underwent chemical reactions such as demethylation, ring opening, and bond breaking, eventually progressing toward lower or even non-toxicity. This process resulted in the formation of three products (CO2, H2O and NH3), achieving a hazard-minimizing treatment.

Mung bean sprouts were cultured with pure water and TC solution before and after degradation with CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN, to assess the biotoxicity of the resulting reaction solution. As shown in Fig. 9c, the mung bean sprouts grew better in the TC solution after CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN catalyzed degradation than in the untreated TC solution, but worse than that of pure water. This indicated that the untreated TC solution has significant phytotoxicity, and CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN was able to reduce the toxicity.

In summary, CQDs/CoO/PMSCN Z-scheme heterojunctions with excellent stability, photothermal effects, and thermal insulation were successfully synthesized using a one-step thermal condensation and solvothermal method. Various characterization tests exhibited the microstructures, surface chemical states, and photoelectrochemical properties of the composites. The results exhibited that the CQDs-2/CoO/PMSCN composites possessed excellent visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance, efficiently degrading TC by 94.7 % in 90 min. Additionally, the removal rate remained stable, with less than a 4 % decrease over five cycles. The Z-scheme heterojunction formed between CoO and PMSCN, along with the hollow porous mesh structure, enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. On the other hand, CQDs offer high light-trapping efficiency, which enhances the photothermal effect and further improves the degradation efficiency of the catalyst for TC. Finally, the intermediates generated from TC degradation were characterized using LC-MS, and the ECOSAR assessment and bean sprout experiments indicated that TC degradation proceeded towards lower toxicity and non-toxicity. The possible reaction mechanism for TC degradation by CQDs/CoO/PMSCN composites was hypothesized by combining active substance trapping experiments and ESR tests. In summary, this research lays the foundation for future studies on constructing Z-scheme heterojunction high-performance photothermal catalysts for pollutant remediation and offers a new research perspective.
